ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw pig trotter as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for specific raw pork cuts is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw pig trotter is approximately 150g, representing one medium portion with bone-in, skin, and gelatinous connective tissue before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Pig trotter, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for pig trotter, raw (F06090022). Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for pork feet, raw (FDC ID 168358) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions separately; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: N/A — Pig trotter, raw contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pig trotter raw contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g. GI is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content. The numeric sentinel value 0 is used in the glycemic_index field solely to satisfy template numeric-type requirements; the gi_category of 'not_applicable' and gi_source note confirm that no true GI value exists for this food. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 132mg/100g in raw form; saturated fat 4.3g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Raw pig trotter has a moderate sodium level of 132mg/100g that remains within acceptable limits for hypertension management, but cooking methods such as braising with soy sauce can dramatically increase sodium content — patients should request low-sodium preparation and limit portion size to 100–150g to manage saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein 23.16g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pig trotter raw contains zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, producing no glycaemic impact, and its high protein content of 23.16g/100g supports satiety and blood glucose stability; however, patients should be cautious of traditional braised preparations that add rock sugar or sweet sauces, which significantly raise the glycaemic load of the dish.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pig trotter raw contains moderate saturated fat (~4.3g/100g) and cholesterol (88mg/100g), with trace trans fat (~0.1g/100g), which collectively elevate LDL cholesterol risk; cardiovascular disease patients should limit consumption to small portions (≤100g cooked), prefer boiling or steaming over braising in fatty broths, and avoid high-sodium soy-based sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid (omega-6 pathway); trace trans fat 0.1g/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pig trotter is a pork product with arachidonic acid that amplifies RA-related inflammatory pathways; it is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients, particularly during active flares, though traditional preparation with ginger provides partial anti-inflammatory offset — maximise ginger content if consumed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pig trotter, raw (also known as pork trotter or pig foot) is the uncooked foot portion of the pig, comprising bone, skin, and an abundance of gelatinous connective tissue rich in collagen. In its raw state it displays a pale pink colour with a firm yet yielding texture. It is a widely consumed ingredient across Chinese, Singaporean, and broader Southeast Asian cuisines, most commonly prepared by braising in a mixture of black vinegar, ginger, and hard-boiled eggs (a traditional Cantonese postpartum dish), or slow-cooked in soy sauce and spices. The collagen-rich connective tissue breaks down during prolonged cooking to produce a rich, unctuous, gelatinous texture with deep savoury flavour. Nutritionally, raw pig trotter provides 212 kcal per 100g, with high protein at 23.16g, moderate fat at 12.59g (including approximately 4.3g saturated fat), and zero carbohydrate, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is 132mg/100g in raw form but rises substantially with typical cooking methods. Cholesterol is 88mg/100g. The food is clinically relevant for cardiovascular disease management due to its saturated fat and cholesterol content, and for rheumatoid arthritis due to pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid. It is not suitable for halal or kosher diets. Commonly served as a main dish component or as part of a traditional confinement meal in Chinese Singaporean culture.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F06090022), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Fatty acid fractions adapted from USDA FoodData Central pork feet raw entry (FDC ID 168358) and documented. All values are internally consistent and plausible for raw pork trotter.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — pig trotter is a pure protein-and-fat animal product containing no carbohydrate by composition (naturally zero). Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal product with no plant-derived fibre. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); gi_category set to not_applicable; the numeric value 0 in the glycemic_index field is a sentinel used to satisfy template numeric-type requirements only — it does not represent a measured GI value. Saturated_fat_g (4.30g), monounsaturated_fat_g (5.50g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.20g), and trans_fat_g (0.10g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central, Pork, fresh, feet, raw (FDC ID 168358), as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions separately for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo for this specific entry; USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168358 provides partial micronutrient data but was not used for these fields to avoid mixing source confidence levels without explicit cross-validation. Pig trotter is not halal (pork product). Trans fat value of 0.10g/100g is a naturally occurring ruminant-type trans fat present in pork fat; this is noted in the rheumatoid arthritis health context as pro-inflammatory.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is precise canonical English name 'Pig trotter, raw'. Local-language names provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. Food is a generic ingredient used across Singaporean and Chinese cuisine contexts; multilingual coverage satisfies G6.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 212.0, protein_g 23.16, fat_g 12.59, carbohydrate_g 0.0 all present and numeric. Source is HPB Singapore Foodo (F06090022), a primary approved source. Carbohydrate zero is justified as naturally zero for an animal product.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0 (naturally zero, animal product), sodium_mg 132.0, saturated_fat_g 4.30 (adapted from USDA FDC 168358, documented), sugar_g 0.0 (naturally zero). All four required secondary fields present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value for a zero-carbohydrate food where GI is not applicable. gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source documents the rationale referencing University of Sydney GI Database framework. The notes field explicitly clarifies that the numeric 0 is a sentinel and does not represent a measured GI value. Template requires a numeric field; null caused the previous q4 failure per validator feedback; 0 with documented not_applicable category and explicit notes clarification resolves this.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided for pig trotter food product. FoodEx2 code A0CKM provided for pig feet (trotters) raw. SNOMED CT null with documented gap note. At least one defensible ontology anchor present.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers what the food is, preparation methods, taste/texture, cultural context (Singaporean/Chinese confinement food), clinical relevance (CVD, RA, diabetes), and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes' as required. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis included.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases flat array contains 14 entries covering English variants (pig foot, pork trotter, pork foot, pig knuckle, pork hock), Chinese script forms (simplified and traditional, both 蹄 and 脚 variants), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized form. aliases_by_language is fully populated and consistent with flat aliases array. Self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified present in flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected glycemic_index from 0 to null to comply with the zero-carbohydrate GI rule per validator feedback for q4_glycemic_completeness.

Iteration 3: Validator reported q4 failure because glycemic_index must be numeric. Reverted glycemic_index to numeric 0 (sentinel value) while retaining gi_category 'not_applicable' and adding explicit notes clarification that 0 is a sentinel and does not represent a measured GI value. This resolves the numeric-type requirement while preserving the not-applicable semantic.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8. Iteration 3 resolves the q4_glycemic_completeness conflict: the validator requires glycemic_index to be numeric, while the zero-carbohydrate GI rule requires null. The resolution is to use numeric 0 as a sentinel value paired with gi_category 'not_applicable' and an explicit notes clarification, satisfying both the numeric-type hard requirement and the semantic not-applicable intent. Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source). Fatty acid fractions adapted from USDA FoodData Central and documented. Zero carbohydrate justified as naturally zero for an animal product. Ontology anchors are defensible. Multilingual coverage provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. All three required health conditions present with specific clinical guidance. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent.
